explicit_text: Eric Liddell was a remarkable runner whose world record in the quarter mile was 47.6 seconds, which he set in the final heat. I remember comparing my own world record of 47.8 seconds to his, and how training methods were different back then, with us working while training and thinking of the quarter mile as a distance race rather than a sprint. I raced alongside Eric Liddell and admired his approach, even though nowadays the race is run much faster with more specialized training.

explicit_text: After the Olympics, we traveled to England as part of our journey back. It was a logical stop since we had to wait for the boat's departure, and there was no other place for us to go. England was also where I met Dolly Netter, an English vaudeville dancer who later became a dance teacher in New York. The trip through England added an interesting cultural dimension to our return.

explicit_text: We returned to France after our time in England before heading back to the United States. The trip back was notable for having fewer athletes and more girls accompanying us, which made the journey more enjoyable. France was a key transit point in our long return trip after the Olympics.

explicit_text: Coming back to the United States after the Olympics was quite an event. We received a warm welcome with a parade up Fifth Avenue in New York City, culminating with a speech at New York City Hall by Mayor Highland. It was a proud moment, even though it wasn't as grand as some other welcomes like Lindbergh's. Returning home meant going back to work and everyday life after the excitement of the Games.

explicit_text: Fifth Avenue in New York City was the route of our celebratory parade after returning from the Olympics. We marched up the avenue to New York City Hall, cheered by crowds, and even chanted for a young sprinter from New York to be mayor. It was a memorable welcome and a highlight of our return to the United States.

The City Hall steps served as a stage for our welcome back home.

San Francisco represented part of the diverse backgrounds of the people on our return trip.

explicit_text: Ann Rathum was a girl I met on the trip back who was from Connecticut and went to college clear across the country. I was quite taken with her, and I even thought I might have found a wife if Connecticut hadn't been so far away. She made the trip more interesting and memorable for me.

She was quite different from the American society girls traveling with us, but I enjoyed her company and the contrast she brought to the trip.

explicit_text: Chicago was home for me after the Olympics, where I returned to work at the Chicago Union Station Company. I saw the station grow to eight stories tall, which was impressive at the time. Later, I worked as an associate editor for Rock Products magazine based in Chicago, traveling around the country but always returning to the city. Chicago was the center of my professional and athletic life during those years.

Chicago was the center of my professional and athletic life during those years.

explicit_text: After the Olympics, I went straight back to my job at Chicago Union Station, where I worked as an inspector of public utilities. I witnessed the station rise up to eight stories, which was quite a sight back then. The job was steady for a while, but eventually, I was let go as the work dried up, marking a turning point in my career.

explicit_text: I worked as an assistant and later associate editor for Rock Products magazine, which covered quarries, sand and gravel plants, and cement. This job took me all over the country, from Georgia to Nebraska and Wisconsin, covering conventions and visiting plants. It was a demanding job that made training for athletics difficult, but it gave me valuable experience and travel opportunities.

explicit_text: I aimed to compete in the 1928 Summer Olympics in Amsterdam and made it through the preliminary races at the trials held at Sesquicentennial Stadium in Philadelphia. Unfortunately, I pulled a muscle in the semi-finals and couldn't continue. It was disappointing because I felt that race was truly mine, and I had trained as much as possible despite my work commitments.

explicit_text: In 1929, I became an instructor at the University of Illinois after a friend suggested I seek a faculty position there. Despite never having taught before, I was hired and found success in academia. The Great Depression soon affected the university, leading to salary cuts and eventually layoffs, but I earned my Master's Degree during that time and continued teaching for many years.
